sentences.text: It's moving.
It is moving, I think.
Good morning.
I'm just fine, thank you.
My full name is Efraim-- E-F-R-A-I-M, Hoffman, H-O-F-F-M-A-N.
February 19th, 1928.
Thank you.
My parents' [SIGHS] name, my father's name, is Samuel Hoffman.
My maidenn's mother name was Helen Berkovic.
And we were born in Zlatry, Z-L-A-T-R-Y.
And that is in the Carpathians.
And it was Czechoslovakia at that time.
Well, we were five children.
We were three brothers and two sisters.
My first brother was Adolf.
My second brother was Herman.
And then I had a sister by the name of Bessie.
And then is me.
And I had a younger sister by the name of Mirela.
And we were about two and  a half years apart from each other in-- in-- in the age.
We had a little house.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father was-- my father was a shoemaker.
He didn't have a business.
But usually those people were working out of the homes.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The-- it was a small village.
It was about 4,000 to 5,000 population.
It was about 30 Jewish families livin' there.
We had a little synagogue.
And each family had about five, six, four kids.
And we knew each other.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were raisin our own poultry.
We had chickens, and ducks, and geese.
We had a cow.
We had a little stable where they could stay there.
And it was a poor life.
It wasn't a  real fancy or somethin'.
But life was happy.
We were going to-- by the time the age came, we went  to school.
It was Czechoslovakian school.
After the school-- that was elementary school.
After school, we went to the Jewish school.
And we learned Hebrew, to write and to read.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The place where I come from is called-- the area was called Maramures.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was no big cities around there.
Khust was one of the biggest cities.
And I was about 30 kilometers away from there.
The place I come from was on the border between Romania, Poland, and Hungary.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was also spoken-- Ukrainian is almost like Russian language.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the house, we fluently spoke Yiddish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The people were livin' there on agriculture.
They were farming, raising potatoes, and corn, and wheat.
And also a lot of lumber yard was workin' there.
There was a lot of lumber factories, because we had a lot of forests there.
And there was also a lot of orchards there with fruit trees and things like that.
So we were living-- we had a little garden in the back in the back.
We planted our own vegetables.
And we also could rent a little piece of land and built our-- raise our own bread like wheat, and corn, and potatoes.
And that was the life going on there like that.
It was a lot of small villages.
Tell me about your mother.
What-- what kinds of things did she do?
And what memories do you have of her?
My mother was taking care on all the five children.
She was cooking all day long.
She was washin'.
And she kept the house nice and clean and see that we are very nice, neatly dressed.
She was a very clean lady.
And that was her job to do.
She was taking care of five children.
That was quite a bit of work to do.
Full-time job.
She was a beautiful, tall lady, a lovely lady.
My father was very nice.
He worked very hard, even late at night.
And he was making shoes and boots and whatever the people were coming in and they ordered.
Because we didn't have stores to go and buy ready-made clothes or ready-made shoes.
The life there is if you needed a suit, you went to the tailor.
You needed shoes, you went to the shoemaker.
Also, there was a lot of-- most of the Jewish people had professions.
There were tailors and-- and-- and-- and shoemakers.
They had a few grocery stores.
And that was the life what was going on there.
And we lived pretty happily in that time.
We didn't know about any, you know, any other things.
The life  was going on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was interesting.
We were growing up nice together.
We knew all each other, all the Jewish families.
We visited.
And we were pretty orthodox too.
We were very orthodox.
We had-- we kept a very kosher home.
We had different dishes for meats and milks.
And [SIGHS] came Friday afternoon, my father put away all the work.
We went to the mikvah.
And we observed Shabbat.
We went to shul.
We [? davened. ?] We prayed.
Also, my father and us where it was bar mitzvah had to pray at home twice a day in the morning like [INAUDIBLE] and tallis.
And that was the life going on there.
The mikvah was-- it was a-- a-- a building.
It was something like they had a water there like in a hot tub.
And it was [INAUDIBLE].
And they had a-- a little water heater there.
And that was heating up the mikvah.
So they would go in there every Friday.
Just it was-- that was the way it was supposed to be in the Jewish religion.
You have to go to the mikvah on Friday before Shabbat.
And was there a ceremony?
No-- no, just-- just going there for the mikvah.
That's all.
And after we came home, my father was a big smoker.
He put away the cigarettes.
He didn't smoke on Shabbos.
And he was hardly wait until Shabbos was over so he could light a cigarette again.
And after Shabbos, after we went to shul, we went home.
We ate.
And in the afternoon, we were visiting each other-- go to the neighbors and things like that.
But we were spread out.
It was a population of about, I would say, 4,000 to 5,000 Christians and about 30 Jewish families only.
Yes, and they all-- they all observed the same kind of a life.
They all kept kosher and Shabbos and the holidays.
So it was like one, big, extended family.
Right.
Right.
Yeah.
Well, we felt different because the Jewish children, we all had payots.
And we all wore the-- the little [INAUDIBLE] because we were Jewish people.
And also, [INAUDIBLE] we played with the other kids.
But it was kind of not too anti-Semitic in that time.
They knew that we were Jewish people.
And that's the way it's supposed to be.
So we were gotting-- we were getting along pretty good.
But all the people probably, they put out sometime some anti-Semitic words and things like that, like call you Jew or something like that.
But most of the time, we were gettin' along with those people until later on when start changing around.
[SIGHS] The part I come from is-- it was Czechoslovakia.
It used to be Hungarian in the beginning.
But in the-- after the First World War, Czechoslovakia occupied that area there.
And it was only Czechoslovakia teachers.
And we went to Czechoslovakian schools.
But when the-- in 1939, the Hungarians came back again when the war broke out.
And they moved out.
They said to the Czechs to leave because that was their country.
So the Hungarians took over that area of that-- of that place there.
The Czechs left.
We had no schools.
It was all full with army.
And they were very anti-Semitic.
They were very mean to the Jewish people.
They came in in 1939.
And they start makin' rules, orders.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jewish people and Jewish children were supposed to wear yellow bands, have a Star of David on the front of the coat.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] They-- Jewish people had to put a big Star of David in front of the house so they will know exactly where the Jewish house is.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then they put a curfew that the Jewish people are not supposed to be after six o'clock in the evening out in the street.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They were not supposed to walk in big groups together.
And they started putting out all kinds of orders against the Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And that was going on for a couple of years like that.
It happened suddenly.
We were not-- we were not-- we didn't know nothing about what was going to go on or what-- what-- what is going to happen.
But then when that started out, we already had a little kind of a feeling that it's no good for the Jewish people.
There's going to be something worse coming up later on.
And there was all kind of rumors.
They use to catch Jewish people in the streets and cut their beards-- the Hungarian army or the-- the police.
They had to report all the time if they wanted to go out of town.
They wouldn't let anybody go nowhere.
And then they start drafting the Jewish, [SIGHS] I mean, the people for the army over there.
So my two older brothers got drafted to the Hungarian army.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they were not exactly in the army.
They were in the Munka Tabor.
That is like a hard labor group.
The soldiers were different.
And the Jewish-- the Jewish guys had also to wear those yellow bands on their arms.
And they were sending them in hard work places to build for the army certain bunkers.
And-- and they were discriminated.
[SIGHS]
They were sent out toward the Russian front.
They were just wherever they were going.
They were sending out the Jewish people to dig ditches.
And you know, the people-- the Jewish soldiers, what they were drafting.
So my brothers were in the-- in that-- in that Munka Tabor.
And they were in different places.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then-- [SIGHS] go ahead.

No, I was just going to say, before we go into the future, I would like to come back a little bit and talk about your parents a little bit more and-- and what they would have told you back then about what was happening.
And-- and did you remember having family discussions about what was taking place and-- and how your parents might have reacted to what was happening?
Yes, they were-- we were always talkin' about it, that they were saying that hopefully nothing is going to happen to us, that we will survive.
It won't be too bad.
But we will-- we will-- we will feel something bad coming up.
And there was all kinds of rumors coming in that this happened and that happened, that this got arrested and that got arrested.
And they were more after the Jewish people.
But we couldn't do nothing about it in that time because our parents always said we are going to stay together.
And we will live together and just see what the future will bring.
Hoping for the best is right.
No, not at that time, no, not in-- not in the place I come from.
There wasn't pogroms going on at all.
But the pogroms started out when the Hungarians came in.
Yeah.
No, no, no.
We were just going to the regular school.
And after school, 4 o'clock or so in the afternoon when school was over, we went home, we had a bite, and then we just went to the cheder.
And that's what's going on-- well, it wasn't too much of school there, because it was only about three years of school.
Because I went into school in 1936.
And by '39, it was already no more schools in there.
Nobody was going to school anymore.
The schools were all closed.
And they were all filled up with the army.
Three years of education, right.
No, no.
We didn't have that.
We were all together there.
And the only thing we were different looking because we were having the prayers, you know?
We wore a little yarmulke on the head.
And-- and-- and we were growing up like that with all the children.
And they knew that that's the way we are supposed to be and the way we are supposed to look.
So you remember thinking of yourself maybe a little differently.
Right.
Right.
But not really being treated very differently.
No, no, exactly.
You were accepted.
And diversity was OK.
Yeah.
Exactly.
Yeah.
Well, we were mixed.
The Jewish children were mixed with the non-- with the Christian children in the school.
We were all mixed in there.
No-no, just Christian and Jewish children all together in the same school.
And we-- they always had to say the national anthem and to sing something.
We all stood up.
And they prayed.
And we were just standing up.
And we didn't have to say anything.
They didn't force us to do that in that time.
Right.
Yeah.
Yeah, then the things began to change.
And-- and it-- it start changing pretty-- pretty fast, too.
We started being hated [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by the Hungarians and also by Christian people.
They were working together with the-- with the Hungarians.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the Hungarians were very kind of-- they were mean to us.
I tell you plain and open-- mean to us.
And they were trying to punish us every minute they could.
Well, the example is that they put the Star of David in front of the house, to-- to start beating up Jewish people in the streets, to put a curfew on.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's an example there already.
What else can we start out with?
No, I was still a young child at that time.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I was not involved in that, in those things.
So you stayed away from--
Yeah, right, but I could see-- I could see those-- [SIGHS] those police catching a Jew on the street and take a knife and just pull the beard and-- and cut it with  a knife and just hurting him.
I saw it with my own eyes quite often-- for no reason.
And--
We start to get very scary.
We were very afraid of that.
It was going to be later on we started thinking about it, what was going to happen.
And [SIGHS] after all those things, it was very scary life.
We couldn't go to the shul anymore.
They didn't-- they didn't let us go certain places.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then it came that we-- I have to start to going into the-- into the ghetto.
And they started taking us away in 1944.
We lived like that till about-- the Germans came in very late to our country.
We were the last ones that the Germans occupied at the town there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The-- it was a tough life.
It wasn't easy-- a scary life.
And then in 1944, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Hungarians told us we have to meet at the schoolyard.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And we will be taken away to a ghetto.
No, the Germans came in first. 1944, the Germans came in.
And as soon as they came in, they went to the mayor.
And they got all the Jewish names from the-- from the-- from the little village there.
Came a bulletin-- and they said that we can take 20, 25 kilos with us-- belongings, food, whatever we want to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and meet in the school yard.
Leave everything the way it is in the house.
And we will be assembling there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And if somebody will try to run away, will be shot-- punished for that.
Nobody is supposed to run nowhere, just follow the orders.
So we all start packin' and taking each-- old and young-- the old  people, the young children, all the families.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We went to the schoolyard.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when  they cleared up all the homes, they searched all the homes to see if everybody is out.
The Germans came in there in 194.
And they took us.
They told us we shouldn't be afraid.
Nothing will happen to us.
They would just take us to a ghetto.
And we will be well taken care of and things like that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They came in-- when we were all at a school yard, they came in with big trucks.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they loaded us up on the trucks.
We had to go in those trucks as many as they could push in one of those trucks.
And they took us to the nearest railroad station.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] From the-- from-- from that-- with those trucks, they took-- they took us to-- they pushed us in in those-- in those freight-- freight trains, like cattle trains.
And they took us to Mateszalka, to a ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were going from all those villages collecting all the Jews putting them on those trains, and those trucks, and then to the train and taking us to the ghetto.
We didn't know nowhere where we were going.
We had no idea.
They just throw us in the train.
And they were screaming at us, and-- and start to beat us, and pushing us.
And we had to do it fast.
They pushed us and those trains in about 80 to 100 people in one of those wagons.
It only had a little window there for a little air.
And we were traveling for about three to four days until we came into that Mateszalka, to that ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was somewhere in Hungary.

And there were coming in troops and groups of people every day, because they were going from village to village to village and collecting and gathering all the Jewish people and taking them out of there.
There was no one village left that they shouldn't have gone and searched and-- and get all the Jews out of there.
On the way to that ghetto, it was horrible.
It was terrible.
It was-- it was hot.
It was no air.
Children were crying.
People were getting sick.
We had no air, no bathroom.
They opened the door maybe once or twice a day just for a few minutes to let a little air in there.
It was terrible-- screaming.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People were getting very sick.
They were-- they were-- they were getting-- passing out because of the heat in there and-- and no room to move.
We were just put in like sardines.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Our parents told us nobody is going-- some of the guys wanted to run away.
They thought maybe run away, maybe we will-- we will survive.
The parents didn't want to let the children go nowhere.
They said, we are going to be together until the end.
Whatever will happen to one will happen to all of us.
And we had to respect our parents.
We did very much respect our parents.
So that's why we were all the families pushed in-- little children, infants, and all kinds of things-- in those wagons.
And we arrived to Mateszalka.
They let us out of there.
They pushed us out in the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there were some kind of barracks-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some little buildings.
It's like tents out of canvas.
And there where we had to stay and-- and wait, see what happened next.
Oh, they were also with-- all the family.
No.
My brothers were-- we didn't know about the brothers after.
I was only with my two sisters, me and my father, and my mother.
My brothers were in the army somewhere.
And we didn't know after we left the-- the town where they were.
Or they didn't know where we were.
We didn't know about each other at all in that time.
Yeah.
No, one of my brothers we didn't know, because the Hungarian were sending the Jewish soldiers to dig mines out in the front somewhere.
And those mines exploded.
And a lot of the Jewish soldiers were getting killed or just destroyed, you know, blowing up the bodies.
So we had a letter from the Hungarian army that one of the brother-- one of our brothers is disappeared.
We didn't know where he is.
So there was rumors that a lot of the guys were running.
And they were hiding out and giving themselves up to the partisans, to the Russians.
So we always thought in our mind that he is alive somewhere-- maybe he run away.
And someday, we will meet.
But we never heard about him.
That was the only time that we had a-- a statement from the Hungarian government that he disappeared.
But we never heard about him at all.
He just-- that was the end of it.
